title: Battle of Tsushima
summary: On 27 and 28 May 1905, in the strait between Korea and Kyushu, Admiral Tōgō Heihachirō's Combined Fleet destroyed the Russian Second and Third Pacific Squadrons, which had sailed eighteen thousand nautical miles from the Baltic. In less than two days almost the entire Russian battle line was sunk or captured; the Japanese lost three torpedo boats. It was the most decisive naval action of the industrial age, ended the Russo-Japanese War in Japan's favour, made Japan the first non-European great power of the modern era, and helped bring on the Russian Revolution of 1905.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The Battle of Tsushima was the climax of a war that had been building for a decade. Japan's crushing defeat of Qing China in 1894 to 1895 had exposed the vacuum in northeast Asia, and Russia had rushed to fill it. Under Sergei Witte, the Tsar's finance minister, Russia leased the Manchurian ports of Dairen and Port Arthur from China in 1898, ran the Chinese Eastern Railway across northern Manchuria to Vladivostok, and after the international intervention against the Boxer uprising of 1900 kept large forces in Manchuria that it had promised to withdraw. Tokyo saw an existential threat to Japanese interests in Korea, which lay directly across the Tsushima Strait, and after the collapse of negotiations Admiral Tōgō opened the war with a surprise torpedo attack on the Russian Pacific Squadron in Port Arthur on the night of 8 to 9 February 1904.

The first phase of the war on land and sea went to Japan. General Nogi Maresuke besieged Port Arthur through the summer and autumn of 1904, taking the 203 Metre Hill in December after appalling casualties and using its observation post to sink the Russian battleships anchored below. On the Yellow Sea in August 1904 Tōgō had already fought off the Russian Pacific Squadron's breakout attempt. In February 1905 Field Marshal Ōyama Iwao's armies drove the Russians back at the enormous Battle of Mukden, a three-week engagement involving over half a million men. The Russian government's response was to send its Baltic Fleet, now redesignated the Second Pacific Squadron, halfway around the world to restore the balance at sea.

Command fell to Vice-Admiral Zinovy Petrovich Rozhestvensky, a stiff, competent officer whose fleet was a mixed inheritance. He had four brand-new Borodino-class battleships (Knyaz Suvorov, Imperator Aleksandr III, Borodino, and Orel), the older battleship Oslyabya, a scattering of pre-dreadnought battleships and armoured cruisers, and a train of colliers and support vessels. His crews, many of them raw conscripts, had trained together only briefly. On 15 October 1904 the fleet left Libau in the eastern Baltic. Nine days later, off the Dogger Bank in the North Sea, jittery Russian gunners opened fire on British trawlers they mistook for Japanese torpedo boats, killing three fishermen and nearly triggering war with Britain, whose new ally was Japan.

Denied use of the Suez Canal in the immediate aftermath, the main body under Rozhestvensky rounded the Cape of Good Hope while a division of lighter ships under Rear-Admiral von Fölkersam went via Suez; they rendezvoused at Nossi-Bé off Madagascar in December. Coal was the single greatest problem: neither Britain nor Japan's other partners would allow the Russian ships to coal at Allied ports, so Hamburg-Amerika colliers ran the fleet's fuel. The armour and hulls fouled in the tropics; morale sagged. At Nossi-Bé in early 1905 news arrived of the fall of Port Arthur and the destruction of the First Pacific Squadron, meaning that Rozhestvensky's ships were now no longer relieving a besieged port but sailing alone into the arms of the Japanese main fleet. A reinforcement of older battleships under Rear-Admiral Nikolai Nebogatov, the Third Pacific Squadron, was hastily dispatched via Suez and joined Rozhestvensky in French Indo-Chinese waters in May. On 14 May the combined force, some thirty-eight warships accompanied by auxiliaries, set course north for Vladivostok. Rozhestvensky chose to take the direct route through the narrow Tsushima Strait between Korea and Japan.

Admiral Tōgō, waiting at the Korean port of Chinhae with the Japanese Combined Fleet, had four modern battleships (the flagship Mikasa, Asahi, Fuji, and Shikishima), eight armoured cruisers, a large flotilla of torpedo boats and destroyers, and a decisive advantage in gunnery training and fire-control instruments recently developed by the officer Katō Tomosaburō. He also had the priceless benefit of wireless telegraphy, then a very new technology at sea, and a screen of scouts strung across the approaches to the strait.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
At 02:45 on 27 May 1905 the Japanese auxiliary cruiser Shinano Maru, patrolling southwest of Tsushima, sighted a hospital ship in the Russian train showing lights and closed to identify her. Within minutes she had spotted the whole Russian column and radioed her sighting report in the priceless message: enemy in square 203. Tōgō received it at 05:05 on the Mikasa at Chinhae, put to sea, and moved to intercept. At 13:40 the two fleets came within visual range in the eastern channel of the strait, on a calm sea with limited visibility under low cloud.

Rozhestvensky's ships were steaming north in two long parallel columns at about nine knots, hampered by the slower colliers and repair ships he had insisted on keeping with him. Tōgō's four battleships and eight armoured cruisers approached from the northeast at fifteen knots on a converging course. As the fleets closed, Tōgō hoisted the Z flag on the Mikasa and had signalled to the fleet the message that would enter Japanese national memory: The fate of the Empire depends upon today's battle; let every man do his utmost. At 14:05, with the Russian van almost within range, Tōgō executed the manoeuvre for which he became famous. Rather than passing on the reciprocal course and firing broadsides in a single fleeting exchange, he ordered his line to turn one hundred and eighty degrees to port in succession, each ship following the leader through the same point in the water. The manoeuvre, later called the Tōgō turn, briefly exposed each ship in the turn to concentrated Russian fire, but placed the Japanese battle line ahead of and across the Russian course, in a classic crossing of the T that let the Japanese fire their full broadsides while the Russians could reply mainly with their forward turrets.

The result was catastrophic for the Russians. Japanese fire, better directed and using high-explosive shimose shells that produced enormous flame and smoke on impact rather than penetrating armour, wrecked the upper works of the Russian battleships within half an hour. The old battleship Oslyabya, hit repeatedly at the waterline, capsized and sank at about 15:10, the first modern armoured battleship destroyed by gunfire alone. Rozhestvensky's flagship Knyaz Suvorov was reduced to a burning wreck; the admiral himself was severely wounded by shell splinters and eventually transferred to a destroyer. The new battleship Imperator Aleksandr III sank around 18:50 with virtually all hands; Borodino blew up shortly afterwards when a magazine detonated. As dusk fell the Russian formation broke up.

Through the short summer night of 27 to 28 May, Japanese destroyers and torpedo boats, some sixty craft in all, attacked the fleeing and scattered Russian ships repeatedly. The battleships Navarin and Sisoi Veliky were sunk or scuttled after torpedo hits; the elderly armoured cruisers Admiral Nakhimov and Vladimir Monomakh followed. At dawn on 28 May Rear-Admiral Nebogatov, who had assumed command of a small remnant including his own battleship Imperator Nikolai I and three of the coastal defence ships, was surrounded east of the Liancourt Rocks by superior Japanese forces at extreme gunnery range. Judging further resistance suicidal, he hoisted the international signal for surrender at about 10:34, ordered his ships to stop engines and lower the St Andrew's ensign, and formally surrendered to Tōgō around noon on 28 May, an act for which he would later be court-martialled and imprisoned in Russia.

When the smoke cleared, the Russian Second and Third Pacific Squadrons had ceased to exist. Six battleships had been sunk and two captured; one coast-defence battleship sunk and two more captured; and twelve other cruisers, destroyers, and auxiliaries sunk. About 4,380 Russian sailors had been killed and 5,917 taken prisoner, including Rozhestvensky and Nebogatov. Only three small ships, the cruiser Almaz and the destroyers Bravyi and Grozny, reached Vladivostok. The Japanese had lost three torpedo boats and 117 men. In tonnage and completeness the Russian defeat was the most one-sided fleet action since Trafalgar.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The strategic effect was immediate. With no fleet left to challenge Japanese command of the sea, Tsar Nicholas II accepted an American offer of mediation by President Theodore Roosevelt. Envoys of the two powers met at the Portsmouth Navy Yard in New Hampshire in August 1905, and on 5 September the Treaty of Portsmouth ended the war. Japan received the southern half of the island of Sakhalin, took over the Russian lease on Port Arthur and the Liaodong Peninsula, was recognised as the paramount power in Korea (which it made a formal protectorate in November 1905 and annexed outright in 1910), and was confirmed in control of the South Manchuria Railway. Russia paid no indemnity, an outcome that Japanese public opinion regarded as an insult and that triggered the Hibiya riots in Tokyo in September. Roosevelt received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1906 for his mediation.

Inside Russia the defeat was politically explosive. On 22 January 1905, months before Tsushima, troops of the Imperial Guard had fired on a procession of workers approaching the Winter Palace with a petition, killing perhaps two hundred in what was called Bloody Sunday. Through the spring strikes and rural violence had spread. The news of Tsushima, published in Russian newspapers in late May and early June, discredited the autocracy still further, and later that summer the crew of the Black Sea battleship Potemkin mutinied at Odessa. A general strike in October forced Nicholas II to issue the October Manifesto promising a Duma parliament and civil freedoms; it was the first constitutional retreat by the Romanov dynasty since Peter the Great and prepared the ground for 1917.

The naval and strategic consequences reshaped the world. Tsushima proved beyond argument that the day of the mixed-calibre pre-dreadnought battleship was over. Long-range gunnery, wireless coordination, and heavy uniform-calibre armament had decided the battle; the British Admiralty under Sir John Fisher already had HMS Dreadnought under design and laid her down four months after Tsushima. She rendered every existing capital ship obsolete when she was launched in February 1906 and set off the Anglo-German dreadnought race that pushed Europe closer to the First World War. The Japanese navy came out of Tsushima as the third-largest in the world by tonnage.

The symbolic consequences ran deeper still. For the first time in the industrial age a non-European power had crushed a European great power in open battle. Across Asia, from the Ottoman Empire to Egypt, from Persia to the Indian Congress movement to Sun Yat-sen's revolutionary circles in southern China, Japanese victory over the Russian giant was celebrated as evidence that Western military supremacy was not eternal. Rabindranath Tagore, Kemal Atatürk, and Jawaharlal Nehru all later described the electrifying impression Tsushima had made on their generation. The victory came at a cost that would prove enormous: the confidence it produced in the Japanese army and navy would feed the militarism that led ultimately to Pearl Harbor and the Pacific War.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
Admiral Tōgō was made a marshal-admiral and a national hero. When he died in 1934 his state funeral drew crowds from across Japan and delegations from foreign navies who came to honour the sailor Anglo-American observers had already labelled the Nelson of the East. His flagship Mikasa, preserved as a memorial and museum ship at Yokosuka since 1926, is the last surviving pre-dreadnought battleship anywhere in the world, and school groups still visit her painted teak decks. Tsushima Day, 27 May, was observed in imperial Japan as Navy Day and is still commemorated in maritime and veteran circles. The Meiji shrine at Yokohama and the Tōgō shrine at Harajuku, dedicated to the admiral in 1940, remain places of Shinto observance.

For Russia the memory was long and bitter. Rozhestvensky was court-martialled in 1906 and acquitted on grounds that he had been unconscious during the surrender; Nebogatov was convicted, sentenced to death, and had his sentence commuted to ten years' imprisonment. The literary chronicler of the fleet's disaster, the ordinary seaman Alexei Novikov-Priboi, later wrote Tsushima, a two-volume novel published in the 1930s that remains a classic account of a doomed voyage from below decks and won the Stalin Prize in 1941. Soviet naval writers used the disaster as a moral tale about the technological and political rot of the tsarist state.

Strategically, Tsushima has been dissected in every naval war college in the world since 1905. Alfred Thayer Mahan, whose ideas on sea power Tōgō had studied at the Royal Naval College at Greenwich, saw it as the decisive fleet-in-being action his theory predicted. Later analysts including Sir Julian Corbett cautioned that Tsushima had been possible only because Russia had exposed its whole fleet at the end of an impossibly long voyage. Both readings underwrote the naval doctrines of the interwar era, from Washington Treaty battleships through Pearl Harbor to Midway, and are still taught in professional military education. The battle also entered Russian naval and popular vocabulary as a byword for total catastrophe, a status it has retained now for more than a century.

Beyond the sea services, Tsushima has kept its symbolic weight for Asian and postcolonial thinkers. Sun Yat-sen wrote that the news of the Russian defeat had made him weep for joy; the young Kemal Atatürk described its impact on Ottoman military reformers; the Indian Congress leaders Bal Gangadhar Tilak and Jawaharlal Nehru cited it as proof that European power could be beaten with European methods. That symbolism, uncomfortably twinned with the militarism the victory would eventually feed inside Japan itself, is the deepest legacy of the twenty-four hours in the strait between Korea and Kyushu in May 1905.

worldContext.text: Sub-Saharan Africa in 1905 lived under the recently completed European partition. The Congo Free State, the personal domain of King Leopold II of Belgium, was at the height of the atrocities documented by Edmund Dene Morel and the British consul Roger Casement, whose damning 1904 report on rubber-collection abuses was fuelling the international Congo Reform Association. In German South-West Africa the Herero and Nama genocide, begun the previous year with General Lothar von Trotha's extermination order after the Battle of Waterberg, continued through 1905 and would leave perhaps eighty per cent of the Herero and half the Nama population dead. In Egypt the young Mustafa Kamil founded the National Party (Al-Hizb al-Watani) that year to campaign against British occupation.

The United States in 1905 was entering the second full year of Theodore Roosevelt's presidency, elected in his own right the previous November. Roosevelt was pursuing a muscular foreign policy: his Corollary to the Monroe Doctrine, announced in December 1904, claimed a right of intervention in Latin America to preempt European debt collection, and the Panama Canal, whose route the US had secured by backing Panamanian secession from Colombia in 1903, was under construction with medical work led by William Gorgas. Argentina and Brazil were absorbing enormous waves of European immigration; Buenos Aires alone received almost a hundred thousand new arrivals a year, driving the city's growth from tango barrios to fashionable Belle Époque boulevards.

British India in 1905 was in turmoil over Lord Curzon's partition of Bengal, promulgated on 16 October, which split the enormous province along broadly religious lines into a Muslim-majority east and a Hindu-majority west. Educated Bengalis regarded it as a deliberate divide-and-rule assault and launched the Swadeshi movement, boycotting British goods and burning imported cloth. The mass agitation nationalised previously localised Congress politics and produced revolutionaries who would trouble the Raj for decades. In Iran, hunger, resentment of European economic penetration, and clerical anger at debt-secured Russian influence would in December 1905 spill over into the Constitutional Revolution, whose Majlis parliament would open in October 1906.

The Ottoman Empire under Sultan Abdul Hamid II remained outwardly stable but was deeply strained. In 1905 Armenian revolutionaries of the Dashnaktsutyun narrowly failed to kill the sultan with a bomb in the Yildiz Palace courtyard. Persian discontent was rising towards the Constitutional Revolution; the Egyptian Khedivate under Abbas Hilmi II was formally subordinate to Britain's Lord Cromer. The first Moroccan crisis broke out in March, when Kaiser Wilhelm II landed at Tangier in a show of German support for Moroccan independence, provoking a diplomatic row with France that was only resolved by the Algeciras Conference the following year.

The Commonwealth of Australia, federated only four years earlier in 1901, in 1905 continued to apply the Immigration Restriction Act's dictation test to enforce a White Australia policy; its early Labor government under Chris Watson had been briefly in office in 1904. New Zealand under Liberal Prime Minister Richard Seddon was consolidating its early welfare state: old-age pensions, industrial arbitration, and votes for women (granted 1893) marked it out as a global social laboratory. In the Pacific islands German, French, British, and American colonial authorities were formalising land seizures and copra plantations; the kingdom of Hawaii had been annexed by the United States seven years earlier. Fiji remained a British Crown colony with a large indentured Indian workforce.

figures.name: Admiral Tōgō Heihachirō
figures.role: Commander of the Imperial Japanese Combined Fleet, executed the 180-degree turn that crossed the Russian T at 14:05 on 27 May 1905
figures.credit: Imperial Japanese Navy c.1905, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Vice-Admiral Zinovy Rozhestvensky
figures.role: Commander of the Russian Second Pacific Squadron, wounded on the bridge of the flagship Knyaz Suvorov and captured aboard the destroyer Buiny; court-martialled and acquitted in Russia in 1906

figures.name: Rear-Admiral Nikolai Nebogatov
figures.role: Commander of the reinforcing Third Pacific Squadron, surrendered the surviving remnant to Tōgō on the morning of 28 May 1905 and was later imprisoned by Russia

figures.name: Emperor Meiji
figures.role: Reigning emperor of Japan since 1867, presided over the Meiji Restoration reforms whose military and industrial fruits were displayed at Tsushima

figures.name: Theodore Roosevelt
figures.role: President of the United States, brokered the Treaty of Portsmouth ending the Russo-Japanese War in September 1905 and received the 1906 Nobel Peace Prize for his mediation

images.caption: Admiral Tōgō Heihachirō, inset over the battleship Mikasa leading the Combined Fleet at Tsushima, from an Imperial Japanese Navy commemorative photograph circa 1905.
images.credit: Imperial Japanese Navy, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: The Battle of Tsushima, a 1905 painting by the Japanese Yōga artist Tōjō Shōtarō (1865-1929) depicting Tōgō's line in action against the Russian squadrons.
Formal portrait of Marshal-Admiral Tōgō Heihachirō (1848-1934), later remembered in Anglo-American navies as the Nelson of the East.
images.credit: Library of Congress, via Wikimedia Commons

sources.title: Treaty of Portsmouth and the Russo-Japanese War, 1904-1905
sources.publisher: US Department of State, Office of the Historian
